analogy: Think of "Atoms and Molecules" through the chapter's own opening images: Tissues in Action Life begins when a single cell divides itself several times to give rise to a large number of cells. These cells gradually form the skin (protection), muscles (movement), bones (support), nerves (control and coordination), and all other organs. This process is so... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Atoms and Molecules is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that tissues in Action Life begins when a single cell divides itself several times to give rise to a large number of cells.

The text explains that these cells gradually form the skin (protection), muscles (movement), bones (support), nerves (control and coordination), and all other organs.

The text explains that this process is so intricate that it is considered one of nature’s greatest engineering marvels.

The text explains that researchers have been trying to understand, replicate and modify this process for human welfare.

The text explains that to do so, it is essential to understand the natural biological processes that govern growth and development in plants and animals.

The text explains that what makes cells group together to form tissues?

The text explains that why do some tissues grow throughout life while others do not?

The text explains that in Chapter 2, Cell: The Building Block of Life, you have learnt that the cell is the basic unit of life.

The text explains that many cells come together to form a multicellular organism.

The text explains that in all multicellular organisms, there is a hierarchy of organisation.

The text explains that cells of similar type performing similar function group together to form a tissue, more than one type of tissues form organs, different organs form organ systems and organ systems form an organism.

The text explains that in unicellular organisms, such as amoeba, a single cell performs all functions of life.

The text explains that in multicellular organisms like plants and animals, different groups of cells perform different functions.

The text explains that a tissue is a group of cells (similar in structure) that work together to perform a specific function.

The text explains that the formation of different types of tissues leads to the division of labour, which increases the efficiency of the body and enables it to carry out complex life processes.

The text explains that for example, Think It Over y How is the study of cells and tissues significant for understanding the life processes and human welfare? y How are tissues in plants and animals different, and why? y How is the division of labour at various levels of organisation in multicellular organisms correlated with their structure and function?

The text explains that in plants, conducting tissues, such as xylem transports water and minerals, while phloem transports food.

The text explains that animals have tissues that help them digest food obtained from different food sources, while plants have tissues that help them utilise solar energy for synthesising the food components through photosynthesis.

The text explains that in roots, these projections are called root hair, which increase the surface area for absorption of water and minerals from the soil.

The text explains that 3.9a) is the tissue that transports water and minerals from the roots to other parts of the plant.

The text explains that use online references or health resources to find average bone and muscle mass percentage for your age, gender, and an Indian body type (these may vary by ethnicity).

The text explains that 3.5.2 Hinge joint Unlike the shoulder, the elbow bends and straightens in one direction only like a door hinge (Table 3.5).

The text explains that reason: The bone ends are shaped to allow sliding in all directions.

The text explains that in a forest, it was observed that one of the trees was severely debarked by an elephant to meet its food requirements, as the bark is a rich source of nutrients (Fig.

The text explains that (ii) Consult an elder community resource persons about their knowledge on different plant leaves, such as leaves that remain fresh for a long time, repel water or deter insects.

The text explains that how would it support bone and muscle health? y Reflect on any gardening methods you know, such as pruning, grafting, irrigation or crop rotation.

The text explains that how does each practice support the healthy functioning of plant tissues like meristems, conducting tissues or supporting tissues? y Turn a nature walk into a research project.

The text explains that (i) Observe different leaves and study their adaptations for various environments, such as desert, very moist or aquatic habitats.

The text explains that find out their traditional uses, such as making plates, preparing cooling wraps or functioning as insect repellents. y Study various dance forms of different tribal communities across the country.

The text explains that each student learn and experience at least five steps.

The text explains that observe the joint movements involved in performing these steps and then develop a dance or drama on the concept of joint movements.

The text explains that perform this at the school’s annual function so that students from different grades can learn from it.

The text explains that 3.24 Will it be possible to obtain a complete animal from an animal cell like plants?

The text explains that if yes, what would be the advantages and challenges of this development?

Source: NCERT Class 9 Science, Chapter 3: Atoms and Molecules

ncert_coverage: NCERT Class 9 Science Chapter 3: Atoms and Molecules
prelims_traps: Do not treat 3.1 Why are Plant and Animal Tissues Different? and 3.2 Tissues for Growth in Plants as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Atoms and Molecules; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Atoms and Molecules through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 3.1 Why are Plant and Animal Tissues Different? and 3.2 Tissues for Growth in Plants to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
